one_line_takeaway: PCFI is a lightweight, pattern-based, priority-lattice (S>D>U>R) middleware gateway that intercepted 100% of attack-labeled requests with 0% false positives and ~0.04ms median overhead on the authors' own 150-sample synthetic benchmark, but the perfect scores reflect a small, self-authored, pattern-matchable dataset rather than demonstrated robustness to paraphrased or obfuscated real-world attacks.
key_finding: On the paper's own 150-sample custom benchmark (50 benign, 100 attack), the No-Defense baseline has APR = 100% and FPR = 0% (latency not applicable). Full PCFI achieves APR = 0% (all 100 attack-labeled requests intercepted, i.e., 0% attack pass-through) and FPR = 0% (all 50 benign requests preserved), with a median per-request latency overhead of 0.04 ms and tail latencies of p95 = 0.08 ms and p99 = 0.14 ms across the 150 evaluated samples.
core_contribution: The paper proposes Prompt Control-Flow Integrity (PCFI), a runtime, provenance- and priority-aware middleware defense against prompt injection in deployed LLM systems. Instead of treating a prompt as a flat string, PCFI represents each request as a structured composition of four segments — system (S), developer (D), user (U), and retrieved-context (R) — each modeled as a tuple (text, role, priority, provenance label, metadata), with a fixed priority lattice S > D > U > R. The defense is implemented as a FastAPI-based API-boundary gateway that runs a three-stage pipeline before forwarding a request to the backend LLM: (1) lexical heuristics that scan user/retrieved segments for override phrases, exfiltration requests, or role-like fragments and produce a risk score; (2) role-switch detection that looks for lower-priority segments impersonating privileged roles (e.g., 'system:' prefixes, '"role":"system"' fields, XML-like role tags) and can trigger a SANITIZE outcome that strips the role-like markers; (3) hierarchical policy enforcement that checks, via a forbidden-pattern set F, whether any lower-priority segment x in {U, R} contains a pattern f in F when a higher-priority governing segment from {S, D} is present, blocking the request if so. The pipeline yields one of three decisions: ALLOW, SANITIZE, or BLOCK. The authors explicitly frame the contribution as a structural, policy-driven/pattern-based enforcement layer rather than a semantic jailbreak detector, requiring no model retraining or backend interface changes.
threat_model: The defender controls the API gateway, the system prompt, and the developer template. The attacker can supply arbitrary user input and can influence retrieved documents entering the request via a RAG pipeline (indirect injection), but cannot modify the middleware, change backend model parameters, or directly alter the system policy. The attacker's goal is to use lower-priority content (user or retrieved-context segments) to override or weaken higher-priority instructions (system or developer segments). The paper explicitly scopes PCFI to single-request prompt composition and states it does not address multi-turn conversations, persistent memory, tool-calling chains, agentic workflows, model misalignment, unsafe training data, insecure tool execution, or downstream application logic.
limitations: The authors state these limitations themselves: (1) detection is largely pattern-based and may be brittle against paraphrased, obfuscated, or semantically indirect attacks; (2) the system covers only single-request prompt composition, not multi-turn conversations, persistent memory, tool-calling chains, or agentic workflows; (3) the evaluation uses a self-constructed, synthetic/semi-realistic benchmark rather than a public one, so the 'perfect' 0% APR / 0% FPR result should not be read as a robustness guarantee, and the reported metric is gateway-level interception rather than end-to-end model-compromise; (4) PCFI addresses only one layer of LLM security and does not solve model misalignment, unsafe training data, insecure tool execution, or downstream application logic — it is intended as a complementary boundary defense.

datasets_used: Custom JSONL benchmark constructed by the authors: 150 samples total (50 benign, 100 attack-labeled: direct prompt-injection and indirect RAG-injection subsets)
quantitative_results: No-Defense baseline: Attack Pass-Through Rate (APR) = 100%, False Positive Rate (FPR) = 0%, latency not applicable
Full PCFI latency: median 0.04 ms, p95 0.08 ms, p99 0.14 ms across 150 samples
baselines_compared: Conceptual no-defense baseline (all requests forwarded unmodified) — this is the only empirical baseline compared in the paper's own experiments; StruQ, NeMo Guardrails, and lexical/embedding-based detectors mentioned in the Literature Review are cited related work, not baselines run in this paper's evaluation
